description: Injection flaws occur when untrusted data is sent to an interpreter as part of a command or query. The attacker's hostile data can trick the interpreter into executing unintended commands or accessing data without authorization.

entries.name: SQL Injection via String Concatenation
entries.description: User input directly concatenated into SQL query string without parameterization.
entries.how_it_works: Attacker provides input containing SQL metacharacters (quotes, semicolons, UNION) that break out of the intended query structure and execute arbitrary SQL.
preconditions: User-controlled input reaches SQL query
No parameterized queries used
No input validation for SQL metacharacters
impact: In some cases: OS command execution via xp_cmdshell or LOAD_FILE
entries.detection_guidance: Find paths where user input flows to cursor.execute(), engine.execute(), or raw SQL methods without parameterization (? or %s placeholders).
exploit_payloads: ' UNION SELECT username, password FROM users --
remediation: Use parameterized queries (prepared statements)
Use ORM methods that handle escaping
Apply input validation as defense-in-depth
Use least-privilege database accounts

entries.name: SQL Injection via ORM Raw Query
entries.description: ORM's raw query escape hatch used with user input, bypassing ORM's built-in protections.
entries.how_it_works: ORMs like SQLAlchemy, Django ORM provide raw() or text() methods for complex queries. When user input is interpolated into these, the ORM's parameterization is bypassed.
preconditions: ORM raw query method used
User input interpolated into raw SQL string
impact: Same as standard SQLi — full database access
entries.vulnerable_code:
from sqlalchemy import text
result = db.execute(text(f"SELECT * FROM audit WHERE tenant = '{tenant_name}'"))
entries.safe_code:
from sqlalchemy import text
result = db.execute(text("SELECT * FROM audit WHERE tenant = :tenant"), {"tenant": tenant_name})
remediation: Use ORM's bound parameters even in raw queries
Avoid raw SQL entirely when ORM methods suffice

entries.name: NoSQL Injection in MongoDB
entries.description: User input interpreted as query operators in MongoDB, allowing query manipulation.
entries.how_it_works: MongoDB queries accept JSON objects. If user input like {"$gt": ""} is passed where a string value is expected, it becomes a query operator that matches all documents.
preconditions: User input parsed as JSON and used directly in MongoDB query
No type validation on query parameters
entries.detection_guidance: Find paths where parsed JSON from request body flows to collection.find() or similar without type checking.
entries.safe_code:
username = str(body.get("username", ""))
password = str(body.get("password", ""))
user = db.users.find_one({"username": username, "password": password})
remediation: Explicitly cast inputs to expected types (str, int)
Validate no $ operators in user input
Use schema validation (Pydantic, JSON Schema)

entries.name: DynamoDB Condition Expression Injection
entries.description: User input interpolated into DynamoDB FilterExpression or ConditionExpression strings.
entries.how_it_works: While DynamoDB's Key conditions are parameterized by design (boto3 Key() class), FilterExpression can be built as strings. If user input is interpolated, expression operators can be injected.
preconditions: FilterExpression built with f-string or .format()
User input reaches the expression string
ExpressionAttributeValues NOT used for the user value
impact: Read data from other partitions via manipulated filter
affected_components: Any DynamoDB SDK with string expression building
entries.detection_guidance: Search for f-string or .format() in FilterExpression, ConditionExpression, UpdateExpression parameters. Safe if ExpressionAttributeValues is used for ALL dynamic values.
remediation: Always use ExpressionAttributeValues for dynamic values
Never build expressions with f-strings
Prefer Key conditions over FilterExpressions for access control

entries.name: OS Command Injection
entries.description: User input passed to system shell execution functions without sanitization.
entries.how_it_works: Attacker appends shell metacharacters (;, |, &&, ``) to user input that is incorporated into a system command, causing execution of arbitrary commands.
preconditions: User input reaches os.system(), subprocess with shell=True, or backtick execution
No input sanitization for shell metacharacters
impact: Arbitrary command execution on the server
entries.detection_guidance: Find paths from user input to: os.system(), subprocess with shell=True, os.popen(). subprocess.run() with a list (not string) and shell=False is SAFE.
remediation: Use subprocess with shell=False and argument list
Use shlex.quote() if shell is unavoidable
Whitelist allowed characters in filename inputs
Avoid system commands entirely — use Python libraries

entries.name: Command Injection via eval/exec
entries.description: User input reaches Python's eval() or exec() functions, allowing arbitrary code execution.
entries.how_it_works: eval() evaluates a Python expression; exec() executes arbitrary Python code. If user input is incorporated, attacker can execute any Python code with the application's privileges.
preconditions: User input reaches eval() or exec()
No sandboxing or restricted globals
impact: Full code execution with Lambda permissions
AWS credential theft via environment variables
entries.detection_guidance: Find paths from user input to eval(), exec(), compile(). In LangGraph agents, check if generated code is executed without sandboxing.
entries.safe_code:
import ast
result = ast.literal_eval(user_expression)  # Only evaluates literals
remediation: Never use eval/exec with user input
Use ast.literal_eval() for safe expression parsing
If code execution is needed: use RestrictedPython or process isolation
For math: use a safe expression parser

entries.name: LDAP Injection
entries.description: User input injected into LDAP search filters without escaping.
entries.how_it_works: LDAP filters use special characters (*, (, ), \, NUL). If user input containing these reaches an LDAP query, it can modify the filter logic to return unauthorized results.
preconditions: User input in LDAP filter string
No LDAP-specific escaping
entries.detection_guidance: Find paths where user input flows to ldap.search_s() filter parameter or similar.
entries.safe_code:
from ldap3.utils.conv import escape_filter_chars
safe_user = escape_filter_chars(username)
conn.search_s(base_dn, ldap.SCOPE_SUBTREE, f'(uid={safe_user})')
remediation: Use LDAP-specific escaping functions
Parameterize LDAP queries where possible
Whitelist allowed characters for usernames

entries.name: XML External Entity (XXE) Injection
entries.description: XML parser processes external entity references, allowing file read, SSRF, or denial of service.
entries.how_it_works: XML allows defining external entities (<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM 'file:///etc/passwd'>). If the parser processes these, attacker can read local files, make network requests, or cause DoS via recursive entity expansion.
preconditions: Application parses user-supplied XML
XML parser has external entity processing enabled (default in many parsers)
entries.detection_guidance: Find XML parsing of user input. Check if defusedxml is used or if external entities are explicitly disabled.
entries.vulnerable_code:
from lxml import etree
tree = etree.parse(user_uploaded_xml)
entries.safe_code:
import defusedxml.ElementTree as ET
tree = ET.parse(user_uploaded_xml)
remediation: Use defusedxml library
Disable external entity processing
Disable DTD processing entirely if not needed

entries.name: Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI)
entries.description: User input embedded in a server-side template that is evaluated, allowing code execution.
entries.how_it_works: Template engines (Jinja2, Mako) evaluate expressions within templates. If user input is placed INTO the template (not as data passed TO the template), attacker can inject template expressions that execute code.
preconditions: User input rendered as part of template source (not as context variable)
Template engine allows code execution in expressions
affected_components: Jinja2 (when render_template_string used with user input)
entries.detection_guidance: Find render_template_string(user_input) or Template(user_input).render(). Safe: render_template('file.html', var=user_input).
exploit_payloads: {% for x in ().__class__.__base__.__subclasses__() %}{% if 'warning' in x.__name__ %}{{x()._module.__builtins__['__import__']('os').popen('id').read()}}{% endif %}{% endfor %}
entries.vulnerable_code:
from jinja2 import Template
output = Template(user_message).render()
entries.safe_code:
from jinja2 import Environment, BaseLoader
env = Environment(loader=BaseLoader(), autoescape=True)
template = env.from_string('Hello {{ name }}')
output = template.render(name=user_message)
remediation: Never use user input as template source
Pass user data as context variables
Use sandboxed template environments
Enable autoescape
